name: Accelerated Idioventricular Rhythm
identifyBy: Wide QRS complex rhythm at 50–100 bpm (between idioventricular escape and VT)
AV dissociation or fusion beats common
No P waves or dissociated P waves
Classic association: reperfusion after MI (good sign - 'reperfusion arrhythmia')
management: Usually benign - no treatment required in most cases
Do NOT give antiarrhythmics routinely - may suppress the rhythm and cause asystole
Atropine may suppress AIVR by increasing sinus rate to override ventricular focus
pearls: AIVR after thrombolysis or PCI = reperfusion arrhythmia - generally good prognostic sign
Rate 'accelerated' compared to ventricular escape (20–40 bpm) but slower than VT (>100 bpm)
Fusion beats (hybrid QRS) confirm AV dissociation and ventricular origin
Usually self-limiting - lasts minutes to hours during reperfusion phase
Distinguish from slow VT (same appearance - clinical context is key)
differentials: Complete heart block with ventricular escape

qaNotes: Slow broad ventricular rhythm without preceding P waves.
